title: Chicken Samosas

overview.description: Crisp triangular pastries filled with spiced chicken, potato, peas and aromatic Indian spices.

ingredients: 300g chicken breast or boneless chicken thigh, finely chopped
300g potatoes, peeled and cut into small cubes
method: Mix the flour and salt in a bowl.
Rub in the oil or ghee until the flour resembles coarse breadcrumbs.
Gradually add the water, mixing until a firm dough forms.
Knead for 5 minutes until smooth.
Cover and rest for at least 30 minutes.
Boil the potatoes until just tender, then drain well.
Heat the oil in a large frying pan.
Add the cumin seeds and crushed coriander seeds.
Cook for 30 seconds until fragrant.
Add the onion and cook until softened.
Add the garlic, ginger and green chilli.
Cook for 1 minute.
Add the chicken and cook until no longer raw.
Add the ground coriander, cumin, garam masala, turmeric and Kashmiri chilli powder.
Stir well and cook for 1 minute.
Add the cooked potatoes and peas.
Cook until the filling is dry and the chicken is fully cooked.
Season with salt and lemon juice.
Stir through the fresh coriander.
Allow the filling to cool completely.
Divide the dough into 8 equal portions.
Roll each portion into a thin oval.
Cut each oval in half to create two semi-circles.
Moisten the straight edge of each semi-circle with water.
Fold into a cone, overlapping the edges and sealing firmly.
Fill each cone with the cooled chicken mixture.
Seal the top edge firmly.
Repeat with the remaining dough and filling.
Deep-fry at 170°C until golden and crisp.
Drain on kitchen paper and allow to cool slightly before serving.

learning.did-you-know: The samosa is thought to have travelled to the Indian subcontinent from Central Asia and the Middle East, where similar filled pastries have been made for centuries.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to make a simple samosa pastry, shape a sealed cone and create a dry, flavourful filling suitable for freezing.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A freezer-friendly snack that brings a classic Indian street-food technique into the Chapman kitchen.
chapman-tips: The dough should be firm rather than soft. A soft dough makes samosas difficult to shape and can produce a less crisp shell.
The filling must be completely cool before filling the pastry.
Keep the filling fairly dry so steam does not split the samosas during cooking.
Don't roll the pastry too thin or the samosas may split.
Seal the edges firmly and remove trapped air before closing.
Fry at a moderate temperature. Oil that is too hot can brown the outside before the pastry becomes properly crisp.
notes.batch-cooking: Freeze the assembled, uncooked samosas on a tray until solid, then transfer to a freezer bag or container. Fry from frozen at approximately 160–170°C until cooked through and golden, allowing extra cooking time.
notes.leftovers: Cooked samosas can be reheated in an oven or air fryer, although they are best freshly fried.
